request.question: What was Debbie Rowe's husband dubbed?

title: Jackson v. Jackson
Lin Huiyin
Michael Jackson
Debbie Rowe
Debbie Purdy
Buy Me
Snarling Beasties
Gallery of Fear
List of The Millers episodes
Paris Jackson (actress)
sentences: Deborah Rowe–Jackson v. Michael Joseph Jackson settled the divorce of Michael Jackson and his second wife Debbie Rowe.
Full custody was granted to Jackson.
Rowe is allowed visitation rights.
She gets property in Palm Beach, Van Nuys and Miami.
Rowe alleged sexual abuse as mentioned in the last amendment in the divorce.
Lin Huiyin (; known as Phyllis Lin or Lin Whei-yin when in the United States; 10 June 1904 – 1 April 1955) was a noted 20th-century Chinese architect and writer.

She is known to be the first female architect in modern China and her husband the famed "Father of Modern Chinese Architecture" Liang Sicheng, both of whom worked as founders and faculty in the newly formed Architecture Department of Northeastern University in 1928 and, after 1949, as professors in Tsinghua University in Beijing.
Liang and Lin began restoration work on cultural heritage sites of China in the post-imperial Republican Era of China; a passion which she would pursue to the end of her life.
The American artist Maya Lin is her niece.
<ref name = "Rowe/Kuan">Peter G. Rowe, Seng Kuan, "Architectural Encounters With Essence and Form in Modern China", MIT Press, 2002, p.219, ISBN  </ref>
Michael Joseph Jackson (August 29, 1958 – June 25, 2009) was an American singer, songwriter, and dancer.
Dubbed the "King of Pop", he was one of the most popular entertainers in the world, and was the best-selling music artist at the time of his death.
Jackson's contributions to music, dance, and fashion along with his publicized personal life made him a global figure in popular culture for over four decades.
Deborah Jeanne Rowe (born December 6, 1958) is an American nurse known for her marriage to Michael Jackson, with whom she had two children.
She lives in Palmdale, California.

Debbie Purdy (4 May 1963 – 23 December 2014) was a British music journalist and political activist from Bradford, West Yorkshire, with primary progressive multiple sclerosis, notable for her challenge to the law in England and Wales as relates to assisted suicide.
On 20 September 2009, it was announced that guidelines on assisted suicide law would be published by the UK Government.

The guidelines for England and Wales "come after a legal battle won by Debbie Purdy", as "Law Lords accepted earlier this year that [Purdy] had a right to know whether her husband would be prosecuted if he helped her to travel abroad to commit suicide."
Buy Me is a television program that has aired on HGTV in the U.S. since 2005, and on HGTV Canada since 2003, where it is that cable network's most popular show.
It is also seen in Belgium and South Africa, either dubbed or with subtitles.
It is produced by Whalley-Abbey Media Holdings (WHAM), which is owned by Debbie Travis and her husband, and produced "Debbie Travis’ Facelift".
The Snarling Beasties were formed in 1986 by Debbie Isitt and Mark Kilmurry and were influenced by the theatre of Steven Berkoff.
After performing "East" by Berkoff, Isitt became the theatre company's writer and director.
Mark Kilmurry played the lead parts in her first plays, "Gangsters" (1988), "Punch & Judy", (1989), "Femme Fatal" (1990) and "The Woman Who Cooked Her Husband" (1992).
The theatre company was very successful and won many awards including the Time Out theatre award, The Perrier Pick of the Fringe (three times) and The Independent Theatre Award.
Their theatre mixed a cinematic style with total theatre and created physical, dynamic and naturalistic plays.
The company was very influential but disbanded in 1996 though Debbie and Mark are both very successful in their chosen creative roles and still plan to do more work in the future.
Gallery of Fear (2013) is a four-part horror anthology, directed by Alan Rowe Kelly and Anthony G. Summer.
Kelly also wrote the screenplay for the film based on a story by Doug Smith.
The film stars Debbie Rochon, Raine Brown, and Susan Adriensen.
The four segments of the anthology are titled "Critics Choice," "By Her Hand, She Draws You Down," "Down the Drain," and "A Far Cry from Home."
The film was distributed by Southpaw Pictures and Tiny Core Pictures.
"The Millers" is an American sitcom that was created by Greg Garcia.
The multi-camera series premiered on CBS as part of the 2013–14 American television season and aired from October 3, 2013 to July 18, 2015.
"The Millers" is set in Leesburg, Virginia where Nathan Miller is a local television news reporter.
His sister Debbie runs a combination yoga studio/vegan restaurant with her husband Adam, with whom she has a daughter named Mikayla.
Nathan does not have any children and often hangs out with Ray, his friend who also serves as a cameraman for the fictional television station at which Nathan works.
As the series begins, Nathan and Debbie's bickering parents, Tom and Carol Miller, are returning from Myrtle Beach.
Nathan informs them he divorced his wife, Janice, several months ago.
Tom responds by deciding to leave Carol after being married for forty-three years.
Tom moves in with Debbie and Adam while Carol moves in with Nathan, each driving their children crazy.
On March 13, 2014, "The Millers" was renewed for a second season, which premiered on October 20, 2014.
Paris-Michael Katherine Jackson (born April 3, 1998) is an American actress, activist, and model.
She is the second child and only daughter of Michael Jackson and Debbie Rowe.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: What was Debbie Rowe's husband dubbed?
hop1_docs: Buy Me | Buy Me is a television program that has aired on HGTV in the U.S. since 2005, and on HGTV Canada since 2003, where it is that cable network's most popular show.  It is also seen in Belgium and South Africa, either dubbed or with subtitles.  It is produced by Whalley-Abbey Media Holdings (WHAM), which is owned by Debbie Travis and her husband, and produced "Debbie Travis’ Facelift".

Debbie Burton | Debbie Burton was an American singer.  She is best known for dubbing the singing voice of the young Baby Jane Hudson (played by child actress Julie Allred) in the 1962 film "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane? ", singing the song "I've Written a Letter to Daddy".  Burton also sang a duet with Bette Davis, the rock and roll song "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?" , written by Frank DeVol and Lukas Heller.  It was released as a promotional single, with Burton's rendition of "I've Written a Letter to Daddy" on the flipside.  An instrumental version of "What Ever Happened to Baby Jane?"  can be heard in the movie.

Betty Noyes | Elizabeth Noyes Hand (October 11, 1912 – December 24, 1987) was a singer and actress best known for dubbing two of Debbie Reynolds' numbers in the 1952 film "Singin' in the Rain."  Today, this is a well-known example of dubbing in a movie musical; ironically, Reynolds's character in "Singin' in the Rain" was supposedly dubbing for another character.

Jackson v. Jackson | Deborah Rowe–Jackson v. Michael Joseph Jackson settled the divorce of Michael Jackson and his second wife Debbie Rowe.  Full custody was granted to Jackson.  Rowe is allowed visitation rights.  She gets property in Palm Beach, Van Nuys and Miami.  Rowe alleged sexual abuse as mentioned in the last amendment in the divorce.
Debbie Rowe | Deborah Jeanne Rowe (born December 6, 1958) is an American nurse known for her marriage to Michael Jackson, with whom she had two children.  She lives in Palmdale, California.

Debbie Boyd | Debbie Boyd (born November, 1957 in Gainesville, Florida) is a Representative in the Florida House of Representatives.  She received her associate degree in building construction from Santa Fe Community College in 1990.  She resides in Newberry, Florida and lives with her husband Tommy Boyd.

Debbi Fields | Debbi Fields (born September 18, 1956) is the founder and spokesperson of Mrs. Fields Bakeries.  Additionally, she has written several cookbooks and makes cookies.  She currently lives in Nashville, Tennessee, with her husband, former Holiday Inn and Harrah's CEO Michael Rose.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Michael Jackson — mentioned in "Debbie Rowe" ("known for her marriage to Michael Jackson") and in "Jackson v. Jackson" ("Michael Joseph Jackson").
  - No other husband candidates appear in the passages.

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - The passages do not state any nickname/epithet that Michael Jackson was "dubbed".
  - No alternative husband or multiple candidates to disambiguate.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - The specific dubbed title/nickname for Michael Jackson (e.g., the epithet he is commonly called).

- retrieval_direction (priority):
  1. Michael Jackson — main biography page (e.g., "Michael Jackson" Wikipedia/title) to find his commonly used epithet.
  2. Query: "Michael Jackson dubbed 'King of Pop'" or "Michael Jackson nickname 'dubbed'".
  3. Sources about his honorifics/nicknames (e.g., "List of nicknames of Michael Jackson" or reputable biographies).
response.hop2_query: Michael Jackson nickname King of Pop
hop2_docs: King of Pop | King of Pop is an honorific nickname most commonly associated with American pop icon Michael Jackson but may also refer to:

List of artists influenced by Michael Jackson | Michael Jackson, also known as the King of Pop, was a pop, music, dance and fashion icon.  His distinctive sound and style has influenced numerous artists of various music genres.

King of Pop (album) | King of Pop is a compilation album by American recording artist Michael Jackson released in commemoration of Michael Jackson's 50th birthday.  The album differs significantly in each country it was released because fan voting was employed to determine the songs for each country's version.  The album's title comes from the title Jackson acquired approximately 20 years earlier.  The album's launch was made public on June 20, 2008, with the official announcement of the Australian version.  The first release came with the German edition (which is identical to the Swiss edition) on August 22, 2008.

One More Chance (Michael Jackson song) | "One More Chance" is a song by American recording artist Michael Jackson.  It was penned by R. Kelly and co-produced by Jackson and Kelly for inclusion on Jackson's compilation album "Number Ones", released in 2003.  The song was later included on the Australian, French, Swedish and Indonesian editions of "King of Pop" and the Japanese limited edition of "The Ultimate Collection" box set.

Cultural impact of Michael Jackson | American entertainer Michael Jackson, who is known as the "King of Pop", is considered one of the most successful and influential entertainers of all time.  He had an unparalleled influence all over the world through his musical, dance, fashion and humanitarian contributions.

Michael Jackson | Michael Joseph Jackson (August 29, 1958 – June 25, 2009) was an American singer, songwriter, and dancer.  Dubbed the "King of Pop", he was one of the most popular entertainers in the world, and was the best-selling music artist at the time of his death.  Jackson's contributions to music, dance, and fashion along with his publicized personal life made him a global figure in popular culture for over four decades.

Unity: The Latin Tribute to Michael Jackson | Unity: The Latin Tribute to Michael Jackson, also known as The Unity Project, is a collaborative musical project aimed at creating a Michael Jackson tribute album inspired by Latin music styles.  The leader of the project is Peruvian-American musician Tony Succar and vocals are provided by a number of leading artists in Latin music genres, especially Salsa.  Succar's aim with the project was to celebrate Jackson's music and legacy by combining it with a Latin-orchestrated sound.  The stated goal is "to honor the legacy of Michael Jackson, the King of Pop.  By remaining true to his artistic essence, excellence, and music we seek to unify all human beings."  Upon its release, the album debuted at number one on the "Billboard" Tropical Albums chart.
hop2_titles: List of artists influenced by Michael Jackson
Unity: The Latin Tribute to Michael Jackson
response.summary_2: King of Pop. Debbie Rowe's husband, Michael Jackson, was dubbed the "King of Pop."
